# Kedro ¶

> [!NOTE]
> > [!NOTE]
> > [Back to top](#code-comparisons-kedro)
> >
> > [View this page](https://github.com/apache/hamilton/blob/main/docs/code-comparisons/kedro.rst?plain=true "View this page")
> >
> > [Edit this page](https://github.com/apache/hamilton/edit/main/docs/code-comparisons/kedro.rst "Edit this page")
> >
> > <a id="code-comparisons-kedro--kedro"></a>

> >
> > # Kedro
> >
> > Both `Kedro` and `Apache Hamilton` are Python tools to help define directed acyclic graph (DAG) of data transformations. While there’s overlap between the two in terms of features, we note two main differences:
> >
> > - `Kedro` is imperative and focuses on tasks; `Apache Hamilton` is declarative and focuses on assets.
> > - `Kedro` is heavier and comes with a project structure, YAML configs, and dataset definition to manage; `Apache Hamilton` is lighter to adopt and you can progressively opt-in features that you find valuable.
> >
> > On this page, we’ll dive into these differences, compare features, and present
> > some code snippets from both tools.
> >
> > > [!NOTE]
> > > **See this GitHub repository to compare a full project using Kedro or Apache Hamilton.**
> > >
> >
> > <a id="code-comparisons-kedro--imperative-vs.-declarative"></a>

> >
> > ## Imperative vs. Declarative
> >
> > There are 3 steps to build and run a dataflow (a DAG, a data pipeline, etc.)
> >
> > 1. Define transformation steps
> > 2. Assemble steps into a dataflow
> > 3. Execute the dataflow to produce data artifacts (tables, ML models, etc.)
> >
> > <a id="code-comparisons-kedro--1.-define-steps"></a>

> >
> > ### 1. Define steps
> >
> > Imperative (`Kedro`) vs. declarative (`Apache Hamilton`) leads to significant differences in **Step 2** and **Step 3** that will shape how you work with the tool. However, **Step 1** remains similar. In fact, both tools use the term **nodes** to refer to steps.
> >
> > <table class="docutils align-left">
> > <thead>
> > <tr><th><p>Kedro (imperative)</p></th>
> > <th><p>Apache Hamilton (declarative)</p></th>
> > </tr>
> > </thead>
> > <tbody>
> > <tr><td><div><div><pre><span></span><span># nodes.py</span>
> > <span>import</span><span> </span><span>pandas</span><span> </span><span>as</span><span> </span><span>pd</span>
> >
> > <span>def</span><span> </span><span>_is_true</span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span>:</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>Series</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>Series</span><span>:</span>
> >     <span>return</span> <span>x</span> <span>==</span> <span>"t"</span>
> >
> > <span>def</span><span> </span><span>preprocess_companies</span><span>(</span><span>companies</span><span>:</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>:</span>
> > <span>    </span><span>"""Preprocesses the data for companies."""</span>
> >     <span>companies</span><span>[</span><span>"iata_approved"</span><span>]</span> <span>=</span> <span>_is_true</span><span>(</span><span>companies</span><span>[</span><span>"iata_approved"</span><span>])</span>
> >     <span>return</span> <span>companies</span>
> >
> > <span>def</span><span> </span><span>preprocess_shuttles</span><span>(</span><span>shuttles</span><span>:</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>:</span>
> > <span>    </span><span>"""Preprocesses the data for shuttles."""</span>
> >     <span>shuttles</span><span>[</span><span>"d_check_complete"</span><span>]</span> <span>=</span> <span>_is_true</span><span>(</span>
> >         <span>shuttles</span><span>[</span><span>"d_check_complete"</span><span>]</span>
> >     <span>)</span>
> >     <span>shuttles</span><span>[</span><span>"moon_clearance_complete"</span><span>]</span> <span>=</span> <span>_is_true</span><span>(</span>
> >         <span>shuttles</span><span>[</span><span>"moon_clearance_complete"</span><span>]</span>
> >     <span>)</span>
> >     <span>return</span> <span>shuttles</span>
> >
> > <span>def</span><span> </span><span>create_model_input_table</span><span>(</span>
> >     <span>shuttles</span><span>:</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>,</span> <span>companies</span><span>:</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>,</span>
> > <span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>:</span>
> > <span>    </span><span>"""Combines all data to create a model input table."""</span>
> >     <span>shuttles</span> <span>=</span> <span>shuttles</span><span>.</span><span>drop</span><span>(</span><span>"id"</span><span>,</span> <span>axis</span><span>=</span><span>1</span><span>)</span>
> >     <span>model_input_table</span> <span>=</span> <span>shuttles</span><span>.</span><span>merge</span><span>(</span>
> >         <span>companies</span><span>,</span> <span>left_on</span><span>=</span><span>"company_id"</span><span>,</span> <span>right_on</span><span>=</span><span>"id"</span>
> >     <span>)</span>
> >     <span>model_input_table</span> <span>=</span> <span>model_input_table</span><span>.</span><span>dropna</span><span>()</span>
> >     <span>return</span> <span>model_input_table</span>
> > </pre></div>
> > </div>
> > </td>
> > <td><div><div><pre><span></span><span># dataflow.py</span>
> > <span>import</span><span> </span><span>pandas</span><span> </span><span>as</span><span> </span><span>pd</span>
> >
> > <span>def</span><span> </span><span>_is_true</span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span>:</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>Series</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>Series</span><span>:</span>
> >     <span>return</span> <span>x</span> <span>==</span> <span>"t"</span>
> >
> > <span>def</span><span> </span><span>companies_preprocessed</span><span>(</span><span>companies</span><span>:</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>:</span>
> > <span>    </span><span>"""Companies with added column `iata_approved`"""</span>
> >     <span>companies</span><span>[</span><span>"iata_approved"</span><span>]</span> <span>=</span> <span>_is_true</span><span>(</span><span>companies</span><span>[</span><span>"iata_approved"</span><span>])</span>
> >     <span>return</span> <span>companies</span>
> >
> > <span>def</span><span> </span><span>shuttles_preprocessed</span><span>(</span><span>shuttles</span><span>:</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>:</span>
> > <span>    </span><span>"""Shuttles with added columns `d_check_complete`</span>
> > <span>    and `moon_clearance_complete`."""</span>
> >     <span>shuttles</span><span>[</span><span>"d_check_complete"</span><span>]</span> <span>=</span> <span>_is_true</span><span>(</span>
> >         <span>shuttles</span><span>[</span><span>"d_check_complete"</span><span>]</span>
> >     <span>)</span>
> >     <span>shuttles</span><span>[</span><span>"moon_clearance_complete"</span><span>]</span> <span>=</span> <span>_is_true</span><span>(</span>
> >         <span>shuttles</span><span>[</span><span>"moon_clearance_complete"</span><span>]</span>
> >     <span>)</span>
> >     <span>return</span> <span>shuttles</span>
> >
> > <span>def</span><span> </span><span>model_input_table</span><span>(</span>
> >     <span>shuttles_preprocessed</span><span>:</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>,</span>
> >     <span>companies_preprocessed</span><span>:</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>,</span>
> > <span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>:</span>
> > <span>    </span><span>"""Table containing shuttles and companies data."""</span>
> >     <span>shuttles_preprocessed</span> <span>=</span> <span>shuttles_preprocessed</span><span>.</span><span>drop</span><span>(</span><span>"id"</span><span>,</span> <span>axis</span><span>=</span><span>1</span><span>)</span>
> >     <span>model_input_table</span> <span>=</span> <span>shuttles_preprocessed</span><span>.</span><span>merge</span><span>(</span>
> >         <span>companies_preprocessed</span><span>,</span> <span>left_on</span><span>=</span><span>"company_id"</span><span>,</span> <span>right_on</span><span>=</span><span>"id"</span>
> >     <span>)</span>
> >     <span>model_input_table</span> <span>=</span> <span>model_input_table</span><span>.</span><span>dropna</span><span>()</span>
> >     <span>return</span> <span>model_input_table</span>
> > </pre></div>
> > </div>
> > </td>
> > </tr>
> > </tbody>
> > </table>
> >
> > The function implementations are exactly the same. Yet, notice that the function names and docstrings were edited slightly. Imperative approaches like `Kedro` typically refer to steps as *tasks* and prefer verbs to describe “the action of the function”. Meanwhile, declarative approaches such as `Apache Hamilton` describe steps as *assets* and use nouns to refer to “the value returned by the function”. This might appear superficial, but it relates to the difference in **Step 2** and **Step 3**.
> >
> > <a id="code-comparisons-kedro--2.-assemble-dataflow"></a>

> >
> > ### 2. Assemble dataflow
> >
> > With `Kedro`, you need to take your functions from **Step 1** and create `node` objects, specifying the node’s name, inputs, and outputs. Then, you create a `pipeline` from a set of `nodes` and `Kedro` assembles the nodes into a DAG. Imperative approaches need to specify how tasks (Kedro nodes) relate to each other.
> >
> > With `Apache Hamilton`, you pass the module containing all functions from **Step 1** and let Apache Hamilton create the `nodes` and the `dataflow`. This is possible because in declarative approaches like Apache Hamilton, each function defines a transform **and** its dependencies on other functions. Notice how in **Step 1**, `model_input_table()` has parameters `shuttles_preprocessed` and `companies_preprocessed`, which refers to other functions in the module. This contains all the required information to build the DAG.
> >
> > <table class="docutils align-left">
> > <thead>
> > <tr><th><p>Kedro (imperative)</p></th>
> > <th><p>Apache Hamilton (declarative)</p></th>
> > </tr>
> > </thead>
> > <tbody>
> > <tr><td><div><div><pre><span></span><span># pipeline.py</span>
> > <span>from</span><span> </span><span>kedro.pipeline</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>Pipeline</span><span>,</span> <span>node</span><span>,</span> <span>pipeline</span>
> > <span>from</span><span> </span><span>nodes</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>(</span>
> >     <span>create_model_input_table</span><span>,</span>
> >     <span>preprocess_companies</span><span>,</span>
> >     <span>preprocess_shuttles</span>
> > <span>)</span>
> >
> > <span>def</span><span> </span><span>create_pipeline</span><span>(</span><span>**</span><span>kwargs</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>Pipeline</span><span>:</span>
> >     <span>return</span> <span>pipeline</span><span>(</span>
> >         <span>[</span>
> >             <span>node</span><span>(</span>
> >                 <span>func</span><span>=</span><span>preprocess_companies</span><span>,</span>
> >                 <span>inputs</span><span>=</span><span>"companies"</span><span>,</span>
> >                 <span>outputs</span><span>=</span><span>"preprocessed_companies"</span><span>,</span>
> >                 <span>name</span><span>=</span><span>"preprocess_companies_node"</span><span>,</span>
> >             <span>),</span>
> >             <span>node</span><span>(</span>
> >                 <span>func</span><span>=</span><span>preprocess_shuttles</span><span>,</span>
> >                 <span>inputs</span><span>=</span><span>"shuttles"</span><span>,</span>
> >                 <span>outputs</span><span>=</span><span>"preprocessed_shuttles"</span><span>,</span>
> >                 <span>name</span><span>=</span><span>"preprocess_shuttles_node"</span><span>,</span>
> >             <span>),</span>
> >             <span>node</span><span>(</span>
> >                 <span>func</span><span>=</span><span>create_model_input_table</span><span>,</span>
> >                 <span>inputs</span><span>=</span><span>[</span>
> >                     <span>"preprocessed_shuttles"</span><span>,</span>
> >                     <span>"preprocessed_companies"</span>
> >                 <span>],</span>
> >                 <span>outputs</span><span>=</span><span>"model_input_table"</span><span>,</span>
> >                 <span>name</span><span>=</span><span>"create_model_input_table_node"</span><span>,</span>
> >             <span>),</span>
> >         <span>]</span>
> >     <span>)</span>
> > </pre></div>
> > </div>
> > </td>
> > <td><div><div><pre><span></span><span># run.py</span>
> > <span>from</span><span> </span><span>hamilton</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>driver</span>
> > <span>import</span><span> </span><span>dataflow</span>  <span># module containing node definitions</span>
> >
> > <span># pass the module to the `Builder` to create a `Driver`</span>
> > <span>dr</span> <span>=</span> <span>driver</span><span>.</span><span>Builder</span><span>()</span><span>.</span><span>with_modules</span><span>(</span><span>dataflow</span><span>)</span><span>.</span><span>build</span><span>()</span>
> > </pre></div>
> > </div>
> > </td>
> > </tr>
> > </tbody>
> > </table>
> >
> > **Benefits of adopting a declarative approach**
> >
> > - Less errors since you skip manual node creation (i.e., strings **will** lead to typos).
> > - Handle complexity since assembling a dataflow remains the same for 10 or 1000 nodes.
> > - Maintainability improves since editing your functions (**Step 1**) modifies the structure of your DAG, removing the pipeline definition as a failure point.
> > - Readability improves because you can understand how functions relate to each other without jumping between files.
> >
> > These benefits of `Apache Hamilton` encourage developers to write smaller functions that are easier to debug and maintain, leading to major code quality gains. On the opposite, the burden of `node` and `pipeline` creation as projects grow in size lead to users stuffing more and more logic in a single node, making it increasingly harder to maintain.
> >
> > <a id="code-comparisons-kedro--3.-execute-dataflow"></a>

> >
> > ### 3. Execute dataflow
> >
> > The primary way to execute `Kedro` pipelines is to use the command line tool with `kedro run --pipeline=my_pipeline`. Pipelines are typically designed for all nodes to be executed while reading data and writing results while going through nodes. It is closer to macro-orchestration frameworks like Airflow in spirit.
> >
> > On the opposite, `Apache Hamilton` dataflows are primarily meant to be executed programmatically (i.e., via Python code) and return results in-memory. This makes it easy to use `Apache Hamilton` within a [FastAPI service](#integrations-fastapi) service or to power an LLM application.
> >
> > For comparable side-by-side code, we can dig into `Kedro` and use the `SequentialRunner` programmatically. To return pipeline results in-memory we would need to hack further with `kedro.io.MemoryDataset`.
> >
> > > [!NOTE]
> > > **Apache Hamilton also has rich support for I/O operations (see Feature comparison below)**
> > >
> >
> > <table class="docutils align-left">
> > <thead>
> > <tr><th><p>Kedro (imperative)</p></th>
> > <th><p>Apache Hamilton (declarative)</p></th>
> > </tr>
> > </thead>
> > <tbody>
> > <tr><td><div><div><pre><span></span><span># run.py</span>
> > <span>from</span><span> </span><span>kedro.runner</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>SequentialRunner</span>
> > <span>from</span><span> </span><span>kedro.framework.session</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>KedroSession</span>
> > <span>from</span><span> </span><span>kedro.framework.startup</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>bootstrap_project</span>
> > <span>from</span><span> </span><span>pipeline</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>create_pipeline</span>
> > <span># ^ from Step 2</span>
> >
> > <span>bootstrap_project</span><span>(</span><span>"."</span><span>)</span>
> > <span>with</span> <span>KedroSession</span><span>.</span><span>create</span><span>()</span> <span>as</span> <span>session</span><span>:</span>
> >     <span>context</span> <span>=</span> <span>session</span><span>.</span><span>load_context</span><span>()</span>
> >     <span>catalog</span> <span>=</span> <span>context</span><span>.</span><span>catalog</span>
> >
> > <span>pipeline</span> <span>=</span> <span>create_pipeline</span><span>()</span><span>.</span><span>to_nodes</span><span>(</span><span>"create_model_input_table"</span><span>)</span>
> > <span>SequentialRunner</span><span>()</span><span>.</span><span>run</span><span>(</span><span>pipeline</span><span>,</span> <span>catalog</span><span>)</span>
> > <span># doesn't return values in-memory</span>
> > </pre></div>
> > </div>
> > </td>
> > <td><div><div><pre><span></span><span># run.py</span>
> > <span>import</span><span> </span><span>pandas</span><span> </span><span>as</span><span> </span><span>pd</span>
> > <span>from</span><span> </span><span>hamilton</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>driver</span>
> > <span>import</span><span> </span><span>dataflow</span>
> >
> > <span>dr</span> <span>=</span> <span>driver</span><span>.</span><span>Builder</span><span>()</span><span>.</span><span>with_modules</span><span>(</span><span>dataflow</span><span>)</span><span>.</span><span>build</span><span>()</span>
> > <span># ^ from Step 2</span>
> > <span>inputs</span> <span>=</span> <span>dict</span><span>(</span>
> >     <span>companies</span><span>=</span><span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>read_parquet</span><span>(</span><span>"path/to/companies.parquet"</span><span>),</span>
> >     <span>shuttles</span><span>=</span><span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>read_parquet</span><span>(</span><span>"path/to/shuttles.parquet"</span><span>),</span>
> > <span>)</span>
> > <span>results</span> <span>=</span> <span>dr</span><span>.</span><span>execute</span><span>([</span><span>"model_input_table"</span><span>],</span> <span>inputs</span><span>=</span><span>inputs</span><span>)</span>
> > <span># results is a dict {"model_input_table": VALUE}</span>
> > </pre></div>
> > </div>
> > </td>
> > </tr>
> > </tbody>
> > </table>
> >
> > An imperative pipeline like `Kedro` is a series of step, just like a recipe. The user can specify “from nodes” or “to nodes” to *slice* the pipeline and not have to execute it in full.
> >
> > For declarative dataflows like `Apache Hamilton` you request assets / nodes by name and the tool will determine the required nodes to execute (here `"model_input_table"`) avoiding wasteful compute.
> >
> > The simple Python interface provided by `Apache Hamilton` allows you to potentially define and execute your dataflow from a single file, which is great to kickstart an analysis or project. Just use `python dataflow.py` to execute it!
> >
> > ```
> > # dataflow.py import pandas as pd def _is_true (x:pd.Series) -> pd.Series:return x =="t" def preprocess_companies (companies:pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:"""Preprocesses the data for companies.""" companies ["iata_approved"] =_is_true (companies ["iata_approved" ]) return companies def preprocess_shuttles (shuttles:pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:"""Preprocesses the data for shuttles.""" shuttles ["d_check_complete"] =_is_true (shuttles ["d_check_complete"]) shuttles ["moon_clearance_complete"] =_is_true (shuttles ["moon_clearance_complete"]) return shuttles def create_model_input_table (shuttles:pd.DataFrame,companies:pd.DataFrame,) -> pd.DataFrame:"""Combines all data to create a model input table.""" shuttles =shuttles.drop ("id",axis =1) model_input_table =shuttles.merge (companies,left_on ="company_id",right_on ="id") model_input_table =model_input_table.dropna () return model_input_table if __name__ =="__main__":from hamilton import driver import dataflow # import itself as a module dr =driver.Builder ().with_modules (dataflow).build () inputs =dict (companies =pd.read_parquet ("path/to/companies.parquet" ),shuttles =pd.read_parquet ("path/to/shuttles.parquet" ),) results =dr.execute (["model_input_table" ],inputs =inputs)
> > ```
> >
> > <a id="code-comparisons-kedro--framework-weight"></a>

> >
> > ## Framework weight
> >
> > After imperative vs. declarative, the next largest difference is the type of user experience they provide. `Kedro` is a more opiniated and heavier framework; `Apache Hamilton` is on the opposite end of the spectrum and tries to be the lightest library possible. This changes the learning curve, adoption, and how each tool will integrate with your stack.
> >
> > <a id="code-comparisons-kedro--kedro-2"></a>

> >
> > ### Kedro
> >
> > `Kedro` is opiniated and provides clear guardrails on how to do things. To begin using it, you’ll need to learn to:
> >
> > - Define nodes and register pipelines
> > - Register datasets using the data catalog construct
> > - Pass parameters to data runs
> > - Configure environment variables and credentials
> > - Navigate the project structure
> >
> > This provides guidance when building your first data pipeline, but it’s also a lot to take in at once. As you’ll see in the [project comparison on GitHub](https://github.com/apache/hamilton/tree/main/examples/kedro), `Kedro` involves more files making it harder to navigate. Also, it’s reliant on YAML which is [generally seen as an unreliable format](https://noyaml.com/). If you have an existing data stack or favorite library, it might clash with `Kedro`’s way of thing (e.g., you have credentials management tool; you prefer [Hydra](https://hydra.cc/) for configs).
> >
> > Apache Hamilton
> > w~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
> >
> > `Apache Hamilton` attempts to get you started quickly. In fact, this page pretty much covered what you need to know:
> >
> > - Define nodes and a dataflow using regular Python functions (no need to even import `hamilton`!)
> > - Build a `Driver` with your dataflow module and call `.execute()` to get results
> >
> > `Apache Hamilton` allows you to start light and opt-in features as your project’s requirements evolve (data validation, scaling compute, testing, etc.). Python is a powerful language with rich editor support and tooling hence why it advocates for “everything in Python” instead of external configs in YAML or JSON. For example, parameters, data assets, and configurations can very much live as dataclasses within a `.py` file. `Apache Hamilton` was built with an extensive plugin system. There are many extensions, some contributed by users, to adapt Apache Hamilton to your project, and it’s easy for you to extend yourself for further customization.
> >
> > In fact, `Apache Hamilton` is so lightweight, you could even run it inside `Kedro`!
> >
> > <a id="code-comparisons-kedro--feature-comparison"></a>

> >
> > ## Feature comparison
> >
> > | Trait | Kedro | Apache Hamilton |
> > | --- | --- | --- |
> > | Focuses on | Tasks (imperative) | Assets (declarative) |
> > | Code structure | Opiniated. Makes assumptions about pipeline creation & registration and configuration. | Unopiniated. |
> > | In-memory execution | Execute using a [KedroSession](https://docs.kedro.org/en/stable/kedro_project_setup/session.html), but returning values in-memory is hacky. | Default |
> > | I/O execution | [Datasets and Data Catalog](https://docs.kedro.org/en/stable/data/data_catalog.html) | [Data Savers & Loaders](https://hamilton.apache.org/concepts/materialization/) |
> > | Expressive DAG definition | ⛔ | [Function modifiers](https://hamilton.apache.org/concepts/function-modifiers/) |
> > | Column-level transformations | ⛔ | ✅ |
> > | LLM applications | ⛔ Limited by in-memory execution and return values. | ✅ declarative API in-memory makes it easy ([RAG app](https://github.com/apache/hamilton/tree/main/examples/LLM_Workflows/retrieval_augmented_generation)). |
> > | Static DAG visualizations | Need `Kedro Viz` installed to export static visualizations. | Visualize entire dataflow, execution path, query what’s upstream, etc. directly in a notebook or output to a file (`.png`, `.svg`, etc.). Single dependency is `graphviz`. |
> > | Interactive DAG viewer | [Kedro Viz](https://github.com/kedro-org/kedro-viz) | [Apache Hamilton UI](https://github.com/apache/hamilton/tree/main/ui) |
> > | Data validation | [Community Pandera plugin](https://github.com/Galileo-Galilei/kedro-pandera/releases) | [Native and Pandera plugin](https://hamilton.apache.org/how-tos/run-data-quality-checks/) |
> > | Executors | [Sequential, multiprocessing, multi-threading](https://docs.kedro.org/en/stable/nodes_and_pipelines/run_a_pipeline.html) | Sequential, async, multiprocessing, multi-threading |
> > | Executor extension | [Spark integration](https://docs.kedro.org/en/stable/integrations/pyspark_integration.html) | [PySpark](https://blog.dagworks.io/p/expressing-pyspark-transformations), Dask, Ray, Modal |
> > | Dynamic branching | ⛔ | [Parallelizable/Collect](https://hamilton.apache.org/concepts/parallel-task/) for easy parallelization. |
> > | Command line tool (CLI) | ✅ | ✅ |
> > | Node and pipeline testing | ✅ | ✅ |
> > | Jupyter notebook extensions | ✅ | ✅ |
> >
> > Both `Kedro` and `Apache Hamilton` provide applications to view dataflows/pipelines and interact with their results. Here, `Kedro` provides a lighter webserver and UI, while `Apache Hamilton` offers a production-ready containerized application.
> >
> > | Trait | Kedro Viz | Apache Hamilton UI |
> > | --- | --- | --- |
> > | Interactive dataflow viewer | ✅ | ✅ |
> > | View code definition of nodes | ✅ | ✅ |
> > | Code versioning | Git SHA (may be out of sync with actual code) | Node-level versioning at runtime |
> > | Collapsible view | ✅ | ✅ |
> > | Tag nodes | ✅ | ✅ |
> > | Execution observability | ⛔ | ✅ |
> > | Artifact lineage and versioning | ⛔ | ✅ |
> > | Column-level lineage | ⛔ | ✅ |
> > | Compare run results | ✅ | ✅ |
> > | Rich artifact view | Preview 5 dataframe rows. Metadata about artifact (column count, row count, size). | Automatic statistical profiling of various dataframe libraries. |
> >
> > <a id="code-comparisons-kedro--more-information"></a>

> >
> > ## More information
> >
> > For a full side-by-side example of Kedro and Apache Hamilton, visit [this GitHub repository](https://github.com/apache/hamilton/tree/main/examples/kedro)
> >
> > For more questions, join our [Slack Channel](https://join.slack.com/t/hamilton-opensource/shared_invite/zt-2niepkra8-DGKGf_tTYhXuJWBTXtIs4g)

# Dagster ¶

[
<svg viewbox="0 0 24 24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<path></path>
</svg>

Back to top](#code-comparisons-dagster)

[
<svg><use href="#svg-eye"></use></svg>

[
<svg><use href="#svg-pencil"></use></svg>

<svg><use href="#svg-toc"></use></svg>

<a id="code-comparisons-dagster--dagster"></a>

# Dagster

Here are some code snippets to compare the macro orchestrator Dagster to the micro orchestrator Apache Hamilton. Apache Hamilton can run inside Dagster, but you wouldn’t run Dagster inside Apache Hamilton.

While the two have different scope, there’s a lot of overlap between the two both in terms of functionality and API. Indeed, Dagster’s software-defined assets introduced in 2022 matches Apache Hamilton’s declarative approach and should feel familiar to users of either.

<a id="code-comparisons-dagster--tl-dr"></a>

## TL;DR

| Trait | Apache Hamilton | Dagster |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Declarative API | ✅ | ✅ |
| Dependencies | Lightweight library with minimal dependencies (`numpy`, `pandas`, `typing_inspect`). Minimizes dependency conflicts. | Heavier framework/system with several dependencies (`pydantic`, `sqlalchemy`, `requests`, `Jinja2`, `protobuf`). `urllib3` on which depends `requests` introduced breaking changes several times and `pydantic` v1 and v2 are incompatible. |
| Macro orchestration | DIY or in tandem with Dagster, Airflow, Prefect, Metaflow, etc. | Includes: manual, schedules, sensors, conditional execution |
| Micro orchestration (i.e., `dbt`, `LangChain`) | Can run anywhere (locally, notebook, macro orchestrator, [FastAPI](https://hamilton.apache.org/integrations/fastapi/), [Streamlit](https://hamilton.apache.org/integrations/streamlit/), pyodide, etc.) | ❌ |
| Code structure | Since it’s micro, there are no restrictions. | Since it’s macro, a certain code structure is required to properly package code. The prevalent use of relative imports in the tutorial reduces code reusability. |
| LLM applications | Well-suited for [LLM applications](https://blog.dagworks.io/p/retrieval-augmented-generation-reference-arch) since it’s a micro orchestration framework. | ❌ |
| Lineage | Fine-grained / column-level lineage. Includes [utilities to explore lineage](https://hamilton.apache.org/how-tos/use-hamilton-for-lineage/). | Coarser operations to reduce orchestration and I/O overhead. |
| Visualization | [View the dataflow and produce visual artifacts](https://hamilton.apache.org/concepts/visualization/). Configurable and supports extensive custom styling. | Export Daster UI in `.svg`. No styling. |
| Run tracking | [DAGWorks](https://docs.dagworks.io/capabilities) (premium) | Dagster UI |
| Experiment Managers | Has an [experiment manager plugin](https://blog.dagworks.io/p/building-a-lightweight-experiment) | ❌ |
| Materializers | [Data Savers & Loaders](https://hamilton.apache.org/concepts/materialization/) | [IO Managers](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/io-managers) |
| Data validation | [Native validators and pandera plugin](https://hamilton.apache.org/how-tos/run-data-quality-checks/) | [Asset checks](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/asset-checks) (experimental), [pandera integration](https://docs.dagster.io/integrations/pandera) |
| Versioning operations | Nodes and dataflow versions are derived from code. | [Asset code version](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/assets/software-defined-assets#asset-code-versions) is specified manually. |
| Versioning data | Automated code version + data value are used to read from cache or compute new results with [DiskCacheAdapter](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/assets/software-defined-assets#asset-code-versions) | Manual asset code version + upstream changes are used to [trigger re-materialization](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/assets/software-defined-assets#asset-code-versions) |
| In-memory Execution | Default | [Materialize in-memory](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/io-managers) |
| Task-based Execution | [TaskBasedExecutor](https://hamilton.apache.org/reference/drivers/Driver/#taskbasedgraphexecutor) | Default |
| Dynamic branching | [Parallelizable/Collect](https://hamilton.apache.org/concepts/parallel-task/) | [Mapping/Collect](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/dynamic) |
| Hooks | [Lifecycle hooks](https://hamilton.apache.org/reference/lifecycle-hooks/) (easier to extend) | [Op Hooks](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/ops-jobs-graphs/op-hooks#op-hooks) |
| Plugins | [Spark](https://blog.dagworks.io/p/expressing-pyspark-transformations), Dask, Ray, [Datadog](https://hamilton.apache.org/reference/lifecycle-hooks/DDOGTracer/), polars, pandera, and more (Apache Hamilton is less restrictive and easier to extend) | [Spark, Dask, polars, pandera, Databricks, Snowflake, Great Expections, and more](https://docs.dagster.io/integrations) (Dagster integrations are more involved to develop) |
| Interactive Development | [Jupyter Magic](https://hamilton.apache.org/how-tos/use-in-jupyter-notebook/#use-hamilton-jupyter-magic), [VSCode extension](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=ThierryJean.hamilton) | ❌ |

<a id="code-comparisons-dagster--dataflow-definition"></a>

## Dataflow definition

<table class="docutils align-left" id="id1">
<caption><span>HackerNews top stories</span></caption>
<thead>
<tr><th><p>Apache Hamilton</p></th>
<th><p>Dagster</p></th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr><td><div><div><pre><span></span><span>from</span><span> </span><span>hamilton.function_modifiers</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>extract_columns</span>

<span>NEWSTORIES_URL</span> <span>=</span> <span>"https://hacker-news.firebaseio.com/v0/topstories.json"</span>

<span>def</span><span> </span><span>topstory_ids</span><span>(</span><span>newstories_url</span><span>:</span> <span>str</span> <span>=</span> <span>NEWSTORIES_URL</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>list</span><span>[</span><span>int</span><span>]:</span>
<span>    </span><span>"""Query the id of the top HackerNews stories"""</span>
    <span>return</span> <span>requests</span><span>.</span><span>get</span><span>(</span><span>newstories_url</span><span>)</span><span>.</span><span>json</span><span>()[:</span><span>100</span><span>]</span>

<span>@extract_columns</span><span>(</span><span>"title"</span><span>)</span>
<span>def</span><span> </span><span>topstories</span><span>(</span><span>topstory_ids</span><span>:</span> <span>list</span><span>[</span><span>int</span><span>])</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>:</span>
<span>    </span><span>"""Query the top HackerNews stories based on ids"""</span>
    <span>results</span> <span>=</span> <span>[]</span>
    <span>for</span> <span>item_id</span> <span>in</span> <span>topstory_ids</span><span>:</span>
        <span>item</span> <span>=</span> <span>requests</span><span>.</span><span>get</span><span>(</span>
            <span>f</span><span>"https://hacker-news.firebaseio.com/v0/item/</span><span>{</span><span>item_id</span><span>}</span><span>.json"</span>
        <span>)</span><span>.</span><span>json</span><span>()</span>
        <span>results</span><span>.</span><span>append</span><span>(</span><span>item</span><span>)</span>
    <span>return</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>(</span><span>results</span><span>)</span>

<span>def</span><span> </span><span>most_frequent_words</span><span>(</span><span>title</span><span>:</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>Series</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>dict</span><span>[</span><span>str</span><span>,</span> <span>int</span><span>]:</span>
<span>    </span><span>"""Compute word frequency in HackerNews story titles"""</span>
    <span>STOPWORDS</span> <span>=</span> <span>[</span><span>"a"</span><span>,</span> <span>"the"</span><span>,</span> <span>"an"</span><span>,</span> <span>"of"</span><span>,</span> <span>"to"</span><span>,</span> <span>"in"</span><span>,</span>
                 <span>"for"</span><span>,</span> <span>"and"</span><span>,</span> <span>"with"</span><span>,</span> <span>"on"</span><span>,</span> <span>"is"</span><span>,</span> <span>"</span><span>\u2013</span><span>"</span><span>]</span>
    <span>word_counts</span> <span>=</span> <span>{}</span>
    <span>for</span> <span>raw_title</span> <span>in</span> <span>title</span><span>:</span>
        <span>for</span> <span>word</span> <span>in</span> <span>raw_title</span><span>.</span><span>lower</span><span>()</span><span>.</span><span>split</span><span>():</span>
            <span>word</span> <span>=</span> <span>word</span><span>.</span><span>strip</span><span>(</span><span>".,-!?:;()[]'</span><span>\"</span><span>-"</span><span>)</span>
            <span>if</span> <span>len</span><span>(</span><span>word</span><span>)</span> <span>==</span> <span>0</span><span>:</span>
                <span>continue</span>

            <span>if</span> <span>word</span> <span>in</span> <span>STOPWORDS</span><span>:</span>
                <span>continue</span>

            <span>word_counts</span><span>[</span><span>word</span><span>]</span> <span>=</span> <span>word_counts</span><span>.</span><span>get</span><span>(</span><span>word</span><span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span> <span>+</span> <span>1</span>
    <span>return</span> <span>word_counts</span>

<span>def</span><span> </span><span>top_25_words_plot</span><span>(</span><span>most_frequent_words</span><span>:</span> <span>dict</span><span>[</span><span>str</span><span>,</span> <span>int</span><span>])</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>Figure</span><span>:</span>
<span>    </span><span>"""Bar plot of the frequency of the top 25 words in HackerNews titles"""</span>
    <span>top_words</span> <span>=</span> <span>{</span>
        <span>pair</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]:</span> <span>pair</span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span>
        <span>for</span> <span>pair</span> <span>in</span> <span>sorted</span><span>(</span>
            <span>most_frequent_words</span><span>.</span><span>items</span><span>(),</span> <span>key</span><span>=</span><span>lambda</span> <span>x</span><span>:</span> <span>x</span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>],</span> <span>reverse</span><span>=</span><span>True</span>
        <span>)[:</span><span>25</span><span>]</span>
    <span>}</span>

    <span>fig</span> <span>=</span> <span>plt</span><span>.</span><span>figure</span><span>(</span><span>figsize</span><span>=</span><span>(</span><span>10</span><span>,</span> <span>6</span><span>))</span>
    <span>plt</span><span>.</span><span>bar</span><span>(</span><span>list</span><span>(</span><span>top_words</span><span>.</span><span>keys</span><span>()),</span> <span>list</span><span>(</span><span>top_words</span><span>.</span><span>values</span><span>()))</span>
    <span>plt</span><span>.</span><span>xticks</span><span>(</span><span>rotation</span><span>=</span><span>45</span><span>,</span> <span>ha</span><span>=</span><span>"right"</span><span>)</span>
    <span>plt</span><span>.</span><span>title</span><span>(</span><span>"Top 25 Words in Hacker News Titles"</span><span>)</span>
    <span>plt</span><span>.</span><span>tight_layout</span><span>()</span>
    <span>return</span> <span>fig</span>

<span>@extract_columns</span><span>(</span><span>"registered_at"</span><span>)</span>
<span>def</span><span> </span><span>signups</span><span>(</span><span>hackernews_api</span><span>:</span> <span>DataGeneratorResource</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>:</span>
<span>    </span><span>"""Query HackerNews signups using a mock API endpoint"""</span>
    <span>return</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>(</span><span>hackernews_api</span><span>.</span><span>get_signups</span><span>())</span>

<span>def</span><span> </span><span>earliest_signup</span><span>(</span><span>registered_at</span><span>:</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>Series</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>int</span><span>:</span>
<span>    </span><span>"""Earliest signup on HackerNews"""</span>
    <span>return</span> <span>registered_at</span><span>.</span><span>min</span><span>()</span>

<span>def</span><span> </span><span>latest_signup</span><span>(</span><span>registered_at</span><span>:</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>Series</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>int</span><span>:</span>
<span>    </span><span>"""Latest signup on HackerNews"""</span>
    <span>return</span> <span>registered_at</span><span>.</span><span>min</span><span>()</span>
</pre></div>
</div>
</td>
<td><div><div><pre><span></span><span>from</span><span> </span><span>dagster</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>AssetExecutionContext</span><span>,</span> <span>MetadataValue</span><span>,</span> <span>asset</span><span>,</span> <span>MaterializeResult</span>

<span>@asset</span>
<span>def</span><span> </span><span>topstory_ids</span><span>()</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>None</span><span>:</span>
    <span>newstories_url</span> <span>=</span> <span>"https://hacker-news.firebaseio.com/v0/topstories.json"</span>
    <span>top_new_story_ids</span> <span>=</span> <span>requests</span><span>.</span><span>get</span><span>(</span><span>newstories_url</span><span>)</span><span>.</span><span>json</span><span>()[:</span><span>100</span><span>]</span>

    <span>os</span><span>.</span><span>makedirs</span><span>(</span><span>"data"</span><span>,</span> <span>exist_ok</span><span>=</span><span>True</span><span>)</span>
    <span>with</span> <span>open</span><span>(</span><span>"data/topstory_ids.json"</span><span>,</span> <span>"w"</span><span>)</span> <span>as</span> <span>f</span><span>:</span>
        <span>json</span><span>.</span><span>dump</span><span>(</span><span>top_new_story_ids</span><span>,</span> <span>f</span><span>)</span>

<span>@asset</span><span>(</span><span>deps</span><span>=</span><span>[</span><span>topstory_ids</span><span>])</span>
<span>def</span><span> </span><span>topstories</span><span>(</span><span>context</span><span>:</span> <span>AssetExecutionContext</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>MaterializeResult</span><span>:</span>
    <span>with</span> <span>open</span><span>(</span><span>"data/topstory_ids.json"</span><span>,</span> <span>"r"</span><span>)</span> <span>as</span> <span>f</span><span>:</span>
        <span>topstory_ids</span> <span>=</span> <span>json</span><span>.</span><span>load</span><span>(</span><span>f</span><span>)</span>

    <span>results</span> <span>=</span> <span>[]</span>
    <span>for</span> <span>item_id</span> <span>in</span> <span>topstory_ids</span><span>:</span>
        <span>item</span> <span>=</span> <span>requests</span><span>.</span><span>get</span><span>(</span>
            <span>f</span><span>"https://hacker-news.firebaseio.com/v0/item/</span><span>{</span><span>item_id</span><span>}</span><span>.json"</span>
        <span>)</span><span>.</span><span>json</span><span>()</span>
        <span>results</span><span>.</span><span>append</span><span>(</span><span>item</span><span>)</span>

        <span>if</span> <span>len</span><span>(</span><span>results</span><span>)</span> <span>%</span> <span>20</span> <span>==</span> <span>0</span><span>:</span>
            <span>context</span><span>.</span><span>log</span><span>.</span><span>info</span><span>(</span><span>f</span><span>"Got </span><span>{</span><span>len</span><span>(</span><span>results</span><span>)</span><span>}</span><span> items so far."</span><span>)</span>

    <span>df</span> <span>=</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>(</span><span>results</span><span>)</span>
    <span>df</span><span>.</span><span>to_csv</span><span>(</span><span>"data/topstories.csv"</span><span>)</span>

    <span>return</span> <span>MaterializeResult</span><span>(</span>
        <span>metadata</span><span>=</span><span>{</span>
            <span>"num_records"</span><span>:</span> <span>len</span><span>(</span><span>df</span><span>),</span>
            <span>"preview"</span><span>:</span> <span>MetadataValue</span><span>.</span><span>md</span><span>(</span><span>df</span><span>.</span><span>head</span><span>()</span><span>.</span><span>to_markdown</span><span>()),</span>
        <span>}</span>
    <span>)</span>

<span>@asset</span><span>(</span><span>deps</span><span>=</span><span>[</span><span>topstories</span><span>])</span>
<span>def</span><span> </span><span>most_frequent_words</span><span>()</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>MaterializeResult</span><span>:</span>
    <span>stopwords</span> <span>=</span> <span>[</span><span>"a"</span><span>,</span> <span>"the"</span><span>,</span> <span>"an"</span><span>,</span> <span>"of"</span><span>,</span> <span>"to"</span><span>,</span> <span>"in"</span><span>,</span>
                 <span>"for"</span><span>,</span> <span>"and"</span><span>,</span> <span>"with"</span><span>,</span> <span>"on"</span><span>,</span> <span>"is"</span><span>]</span>
    <span>topstories</span> <span>=</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>read_csv</span><span>(</span><span>"data/topstories.csv"</span><span>)</span>

    <span>word_counts</span> <span>=</span> <span>{}</span>
    <span>for</span> <span>raw_title</span> <span>in</span> <span>topstories</span><span>[</span><span>"title"</span><span>]:</span>
        <span>title</span> <span>=</span> <span>raw_title</span><span>.</span><span>lower</span><span>()</span>
        <span>for</span> <span>word</span> <span>in</span> <span>title</span><span>.</span><span>split</span><span>():</span>
            <span>word</span> <span>=</span> <span>word</span><span>.</span><span>strip</span><span>(</span><span>".,-!?:;()[]'</span><span>\"</span><span>-"</span><span>)</span>
            <span>if</span> <span>cleaned_word</span> <span>in</span> <span>stopwords</span> <span>or</span> <span>len</span><span>(</span><span>cleaned_word</span><span>)</span> <span>&lt;</span> <span>0</span><span>:</span>
                <span>continue</span>

            <span>word_counts</span><span>[</span><span>cleaned_word</span><span>]</span> <span>=</span> <span>word_counts</span><span>.</span><span>get</span><span>(</span><span>word</span><span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span> <span>+</span> <span>1</span>

    <span>top_words</span> <span>=</span> <span>{</span>
        <span>pair</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]:</span> <span>pair</span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span>
        <span>for</span> <span>pair</span> <span>in</span> <span>sorted</span><span>(</span>
            <span>word_counts</span><span>.</span><span>items</span><span>(),</span> <span>key</span><span>=</span><span>lambda</span> <span>x</span><span>:</span> <span>x</span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>],</span> <span>reverse</span><span>=</span><span>True</span>
        <span>)[:</span><span>25</span><span>]</span>
    <span>}</span>

    <span>plt</span><span>.</span><span>figure</span><span>(</span><span>figsize</span><span>=</span><span>(</span><span>10</span><span>,</span> <span>6</span><span>))</span>
    <span>plt</span><span>.</span><span>bar</span><span>(</span><span>list</span><span>(</span><span>top_words</span><span>.</span><span>keys</span><span>()),</span> <span>list</span><span>(</span><span>top_words</span><span>.</span><span>values</span><span>()))</span>
    <span>plt</span><span>.</span><span>xticks</span><span>(</span><span>rotation</span><span>=</span><span>45</span><span>,</span> <span>ha</span><span>=</span><span>"right"</span><span>)</span>
    <span>plt</span><span>.</span><span>title</span><span>(</span><span>"Top 25 Words in Hacker News Titles"</span><span>)</span>
    <span>plt</span><span>.</span><span>tight_layout</span><span>()</span>

    <span>buffer</span> <span>=</span> <span>BytesIO</span><span>()</span>
    <span>plt</span><span>.</span><span>savefig</span><span>(</span><span>buffer</span><span>,</span> <span>format</span><span>=</span><span>"png"</span><span>)</span>
    <span>image_data</span> <span>=</span> <span>base64</span><span>.</span><span>b64encode</span><span>(</span><span>buffer</span><span>.</span><span>getvalue</span><span>())</span>

    <span>md_content</span> <span>=</span> <span>f</span><span>"![img](data:image/png;base64,</span><span>{</span><span>image_data</span><span>.</span><span>decode</span><span>()</span><span>}</span><span>)"</span>

    <span>with</span> <span>open</span><span>(</span><span>"data/most_frequent_words.json"</span><span>,</span> <span>"w"</span><span>)</span> <span>as</span> <span>f</span><span>:</span>
        <span>json</span><span>.</span><span>dump</span><span>(</span><span>top_words</span><span>,</span> <span>f</span><span>)</span>

    <span>return</span> <span>MaterializeResult</span><span>(</span>
        <span>metadata</span><span>=</span><span>{</span><span>"plot"</span><span>:</span> <span>MetadataValue</span><span>.</span><span>md</span><span>(</span><span>md_content</span><span>)}</span>
    <span>)</span>

<span>@asset</span>
<span>def</span><span> </span><span>signups</span><span>(</span><span>hackernews_api</span><span>:</span> <span>DataGeneratorResource</span><span>)</span> <span>-&gt;</span> <span>MaterializeResult</span><span>:</span>
    <span>signups</span> <span>=</span> <span>pd</span><span>.</span><span>DataFrame</span><span>(</span><span>hackernews_api</span><span>.</span><span>get_signups</span><span>())</span>

    <span>signups</span><span>.</span><span>to_csv</span><span>(</span><span>"data/signups.csv"</span><span>)</span>

    <span>return</span> <span>MaterializeResult</span><span>(</span>
        <span>metadata</span><span>=</span><span>{</span>
            <span>"Record Count"</span><span>:</span> <span>len</span><span>(</span><span>signups</span><span>),</span>
            <span>"Preview"</span><span>:</span> <span>MetadataValue</span><span>.</span><span>md</span><span>(</span><span>signups</span><span>.</span><span>head</span><span>()</span><span>.</span><span>to_markdown</span><span>()),</span>
            <span>"Earliest Signup"</span><span>:</span> <span>signups</span><span>[</span><span>"registered_at"</span><span>]</span><span>.</span><span>min</span><span>(),</span>
            <span>"Latest Signup"</span><span>:</span> <span>signups</span><span>[</span><span>"registered_at"</span><span>]</span><span>.</span><span>max</span><span>(),</span>
        <span>}</span>
    <span>)</span>
</pre></div>
</div>
</td>
</tr>
<tr><td><img alt="../../_images/hamilton_dataflow.png" src="assets/images/hamilton-dataflow_72725e9e6f207c7b.png"/>
</td>
<td><img alt="../../_images/dagster_dataflow.png" src="assets/images/dagster-dataflow_a35c90fa3b7ca8d1.png"/>
</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

| Trait | Apache Hamilton | Dagster |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Define operations | Uses the native Python function signature. The dataflow is assembled based on function/parameter names and type annotations. | Uses the `@asset` decorator to transform function in operations and specify dependencies by passing functions. |
| Data I/O | Loading/Saving is decoupled from the dataflow definition. The code becomes [more portable and facilitates moving from dev to prod](https://blog.dagworks.io/p/separate-data-io-from-transformation). | Each asset code operations is coupled with I/O. Hard-coding this behavior reduces maintainability. |
| Lineage | Favors granular operations and fine-grained lineage. For example, `most_frequent_words()` operates on a single column and the `top_25_words_plot` is its own function. | Favors chunking dataflow into meaningful assets to reduce the orchestration and I/O overhead per operation. Finer lineage is complex to achieve and requires using `@op`, `@graph`, `@job`, and `@asset` ([ref](https://docs.dagster.io/guides/dagster/how-assets-relate-to-ops-and-graphs)) |
| Documentation | Uses the native Python docstrings. Further metadata can be added using the `@tag` decorator. | Uses `MaterializeResult` to store metadata. |

<a id="code-comparisons-dagster--dataflow-execution"></a>

## Dataflow execution

<table class="docutils align-left" id="id3">
<caption><span>HackerNews top stories</span></caption>
<thead>
<tr><th><p>Apache Hamilton</p></th>
<th><p>Dagster</p></th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr><td><div><div><pre><span></span><span>import</span><span> </span><span>os</span>

<span>from</span><span> </span><span>hamilton</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>driver</span>
<span>from</span><span> </span><span>hamilton.io.materialization</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>to</span>
<span>from</span><span> </span><span>hamilton.plugins</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>matplotlib_extensions</span>

<span>import</span><span> </span><span>dataflow</span>  <span># import module with dataflow definition</span>
<span>from</span><span> </span><span>mock_api</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>DataGeneratorResource</span>

<span>def</span><span> </span><span>main</span><span>():</span>
    <span>dr</span> <span>=</span> <span>(</span>
        <span>driver</span><span>.</span><span>Builder</span><span>()</span>
        <span>.</span><span>with_modules</span><span>(</span><span>dataflow</span><span>)</span>  <span># pass the module</span>
        <span>.</span><span>build</span><span>()</span>
    <span>)</span>

    <span># load environment variable</span>
    <span>num_days</span> <span>=</span> <span>os</span><span>.</span><span>environ</span><span>.</span><span>get</span><span>(</span><span>"HACKERNEWS_NUM_DAYS_WINDOW"</span><span>)</span>
    <span>inputs</span> <span>=</span> <span>dict</span><span>(</span> <span># mock an API connection</span>
        <span>hackernews_api</span><span>=</span><span>DataGeneratorResource</span><span>(</span><span>num_days</span><span>=</span><span>num_days</span><span>),</span>
    <span>)</span>

    <span># define I/O operations; decoupled from dataflow def</span>
    <span>materializers</span> <span>=</span> <span>[</span>
        <span>to</span><span>.</span><span>json</span><span>(</span>  <span># JSON file type</span>
            <span>id</span><span>=</span><span>"most_frequent_words.json"</span><span>,</span>
            <span>dependencies</span><span>=</span><span>[</span><span>"most_frequent_words"</span><span>],</span>
            <span>path</span><span>=</span><span>"data/most_frequent_words.json"</span><span>,</span>
        <span>),</span>
        <span>to</span><span>.</span><span>csv</span><span>(</span>  <span># CSV file type</span>
            <span>id</span><span>=</span><span>"topstories.csv"</span><span>,</span>
            <span>dependencies</span><span>=</span><span>[</span><span>"topstories"</span><span>],</span>
            <span>path</span><span>=</span><span>"data/topstories.csv"</span><span>,</span>
        <span>),</span>
        <span>to</span><span>.</span><span>csv</span><span>(</span>
            <span>id</span><span>=</span><span>"signups.csv"</span><span>,</span>
            <span>dependencies</span><span>=</span><span>[</span><span>"signups"</span><span>],</span>
            <span>path</span><span>=</span><span>"data/signups.csv"</span><span>,</span>
        <span>),</span>
        <span>to</span><span>.</span><span>plt</span><span>(</span>  <span># Use matplotlib.pyplot to render</span>
            <span>id</span><span>=</span><span>"top_25_words_plot.plt"</span><span>,</span>
            <span>dependencies</span><span>=</span><span>[</span><span>"top_25_words_plot"</span><span>],</span>
            <span>path</span><span>=</span><span>"data/top_25_words_plot.png"</span><span>,</span>
        <span>),</span>
    <span>]</span>

    <span># visualize materialization plan without executing code</span>
    <span>dr</span><span>.</span><span>visualize_materialization</span><span>(</span>
        <span>*</span><span>materializers</span><span>,</span>
        <span>inputs</span><span>=</span><span>inputs</span><span>,</span>
        <span>output_file_path</span><span>=</span><span>"dataflow.png"</span>
    <span>)</span>
    <span># pass I/O operations and inputs to materialize dataflow</span>
    <span>dr</span><span>.</span><span>materialize</span><span>(</span><span>*</span><span>materializers</span><span>,</span> <span>inputs</span><span>=</span><span>inputs</span><span>)</span>

<span>if</span> <span>__name__</span> <span>==</span> <span>"__main__"</span><span>:</span>
    <span>main</span><span>()</span>

</pre></div>
</div>
</td>
<td><div><div><pre><span></span><span>from</span><span> </span><span>dagster</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>(</span>
    <span>AssetSelection</span><span>,</span>
    <span>Definitions</span><span>,</span>
    <span>define_asset_job</span><span>,</span>
    <span>load_assets_from_modules</span><span>,</span>
    <span>EnvVar</span><span>,</span>
<span>)</span>

<span>from</span><span> </span><span>.</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>assets</span>
<span>from</span><span> </span><span>.resources</span><span> </span><span>import</span> <span>DataGeneratorResource</span>

<span># load assets from passed modules</span>
<span>all_assets</span> <span>=</span> <span>load_assets_from_modules</span><span>([</span><span>assets</span><span>])</span>
<span># select assets to include in the job</span>
<span>hackernews_job</span> <span>=</span> <span>define_asset_job</span><span>(</span><span>"hackernews_job"</span><span>,</span> <span>selection</span><span>=</span><span>AssetSelection</span><span>.</span><span>all</span><span>())</span>

<span># load environment variable</span>
<span>num_days</span> <span>=</span> <span>EnvVar</span><span>.</span><span>int</span><span>(</span><span>"HACKERNEWS_NUM_DAYS_WINDOW"</span><span>)</span>
<span>defs</span> <span>=</span> <span>Definitions</span><span>(</span>
    <span>assets</span><span>=</span><span>all_assets</span><span>,</span>
    <span>jobs</span><span>=</span><span>[</span><span>hackernews_job</span><span>],</span>
    <span>resources</span><span>=</span><span>{</span>  <span># register mock API connection</span>
        <span>"hackernews_api"</span><span>:</span> <span>DataGeneratorResource</span><span>(</span><span>num_days</span><span>=</span><span>num_days</span><span>),</span>
    <span>},</span>
<span>)</span>
</pre></div>
</div>
</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

| Trait | Apache Hamilton | Dagster |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Execution instructions | Define a `Driver` using the `Builder` object. It automatically assembles the graph from the dataflow definition found in `dataflow.py` | Load assets from Python modules using `load_assets_from_modules` then create an asset job by selecting assets to include. Finally, create a `Definitions` object to register on the orchestrator. |
| Execution plane | `Driver.materialize()` executes the dataflow in a Python process. Can be called as a script, [using the CLI](https://blog.dagworks.io/p/a-command-line-tool-to-improve-your), or programmatically. | The [asset job is executed by the orchestrator](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/assets/asset-jobs), either through Dagster UI, by a scheduler/sensor/trigger, or via the CLI. |
| Data I/O | I/O is decoupled from dataflow definition. People responsible for deployment can manage data sources without refactoring the dataflow. (Data I/O can be coupled if wanted.) | Data I/O is coupled with data assets which simplifies the execution code at the code of reusability. |
| Framework code | Leverages a maximum of standard Python mechanisms (imports, env variables, etc.). | Most constructs requires Dagster-specific code to leverage protobuf serialization. |

<a id="code-comparisons-dagster--more-information"></a>

# LangChain ¶
